title: Cognitive & Developmental Analysis
description: Deepen your research portfolio by analyzing your chosen psychological topic through the lenses of sensation and perception, learning theory, cognitive processes, memory, and human development.

context.purpose: This second assignment builds on your topic selection from Assignment 1. You will now examine how cognitive and developmental processes relate to your psychological topic, integrating concepts from Chapters 5-9.
goals: Analyze how sensation and perception relate to your topic
Apply learning theories (classical conditioning, operant conditioning, observational learning) to your topic
Explore cognitive and memory processes involved in your topic
Examine your topic through a developmental lifespan perspective
context.whatToExpect: Each section applies a different cognitive or developmental framework to your topic. The AI coach will help you integrate course concepts and strengthen your analysis.

sections.title: Sensation & Perception Analysis
sections.instructions: Examine how sensation and perception relate to your psychological topic. Consider how sensory processing, perceptual organization (e.g., Gestalt principles), attention, or perceptual illusions connect to your phenomenon. Discuss how individual differences in perception might influence experiences related to your topic.
sections.rubric: Student should correctly apply sensation and perception concepts, demonstrate understanding of the difference between sensation and perception, make specific connections to their topic, and discuss how perceptual processes influence the psychological phenomenon they are studying.
tips: Consider both bottom-up and top-down processing in relation to your topic
Think about how attention and selective perception play a role
Discuss specific sensory modalities (vision, hearing, etc.) if relevant

sections.title: Learning Theory Applications
sections.instructions: Apply the major learning theories from Chapter 6 to your psychological topic. Discuss how classical conditioning, operant conditioning, and/or observational learning (modeling) might explain aspects of your topic. Provide specific examples of how these learning processes could shape behaviors or responses related to your phenomenon.
sections.rubric: Student should accurately describe at least two learning theories and apply them to their topic with specific, realistic examples. Should use correct terminology (unconditioned stimulus, reinforcement, modeling, etc.) and demonstrate understanding of how learning processes shape behavior related to their topic.
tips: Use specific examples — describe what the CS, UCS, CR, and UCR would be if classical conditioning applies
Consider how reinforcement schedules might maintain behaviors related to your topic
Think about role models and media influences if observational learning is relevant

sections.title: Cognition, Intelligence & Memory
sections.instructions: Analyze the cognitive processes and memory mechanisms involved in your psychological topic. Draw on concepts from Chapters 7 and 8, such as problem solving, decision making, language, intelligence, memory encoding, storage, retrieval, or memory errors. Explain how cognitive and memory processes influence or are influenced by your topic.
sections.rubric: Student should integrate concepts from both cognition and memory chapters, correctly use terminology (e.g., schemas, heuristics, encoding, retrieval, working memory), and provide thoughtful analysis of how cognitive processes interact with their topic. Should go beyond surface-level connections.
tips: Consider how cognitive biases or heuristics might affect thinking about your topic
Discuss which memory systems (sensory, short-term, long-term) are most relevant
Think about how memory distortions or failures relate to your topic

sections.title: Lifespan Development Perspective
sections.instructions: Examine your psychological topic through the lens of lifespan development (Chapter 9). Discuss how your topic manifests differently across various life stages (prenatal, infancy, childhood, adolescence, adulthood, late adulthood). Apply at least one developmental theory (e.g., Piaget, Erikson, Kohlberg) to explain how understanding of or experience with your topic changes across the lifespan.
sections.rubric: Student should discuss at least two different life stages, correctly apply a developmental theory, and show how the psychological topic evolves or changes across the lifespan. Should demonstrate understanding that development is a continuous process influenced by biological, cognitive, and social factors.
tips: Consider how your topic might first emerge in development and how it changes over time
Apply a specific developmental theory rather than speaking generally
Think about critical periods or sensitive periods if relevant to your topic
